content.text: An embedding model converts a text string into a dense vector — a list of floating-point numbers — where the position in high-dimensional space encodes semantic meaning. Two texts with similar meaning produce vectors that point in similar directions (high cosine similarity); unrelated texts produce vectors that point in different directions (low cosine similarity). This is what enables semantic search: a query vector is compared against all document vectors, and the closest ones are returned regardless of exact word overlap.

attrs.alt: Text embedding: chunk to dense vector, cosine similarity showing semantically close vectors have small angles
attrs.title: Text Embedding and Cosine Similarity

content.text: LangChain's OpenAIEmbeddings wraps the OpenAI embeddings API and provides two methods: embed_query (for a single query string at retrieval time) and embed_documents (for batching multiple document strings at indexing time). Both return float lists. The distinction matters: some embedding models apply different normalisation to query versus document vectors.

# Embedding documents with OpenAI and LangChain
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_core.documents import Document

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# Embed a single string
vec = embeddings.embed_query("What is the return policy?")
print(f"Query vector: {len(vec)} dims, first 5: {vec[:5]}")

# Embed multiple documents (batched automatically)
docs = [
    "Items may be returned within 30 days with original receipt.",
    "Standard shipping takes 5-7 business days.",
    "Enterprise pricing requires contacting our sales team.",
]
doc_vecs = embeddings.embed_documents(docs)
print(f"Embedded {len(doc_vecs)} docs, each {len(doc_vecs[0])} dims")

# Cosine similarity (manual, without a vector store)
import numpy as np

def cosine_sim(a, b):
    a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b)
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b)))

query_vec = embeddings.embed_query("Can I return a purchase?")
for i, (text, doc_vec) in enumerate(zip(docs, doc_vecs)):
    sim = cosine_sim(query_vec, doc_vec)
    print(f"  [{i}] sim={sim:.3f} | {text[:60]}")
# [0] sim=0.879 | Items may be returned within 30 days...
# [1] sim=0.234 | Standard shipping takes 5-7 business days...
# [2] sim=0.198 | Enterprise pricing requires...

For most RAG pipelines, text-embedding-3-small is the right choice: it produces 1536-dimensional vectors at $0.02 per million tokens and matches text-embedding-3-large on most benchmarks at one-sixth the cost. text-embedding-3-large is worth evaluating when precision on domain-specific or multilingual content is critical. Voyage-3 and Cohere Embed v3 are strong alternatives when OpenAI is not preferred.

# Comparing embedding models: dimension size and retrieval quality
# Larger dimensions ≠ better quality; model architecture matters more.

from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
import numpy as np

def cosine_sim(a, b):
    a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b)
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b)))

# text-embedding-3-small: 1536 dims, $0.02/1M tokens — best cost/quality ratio
small = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# text-embedding-3-large: 3072 dims, $0.13/1M tokens — higher quality for hard cases
large = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-large")

docs = [
    "The product ships within 2 business days.",
    "Returns are accepted within 30 days.",
    "Our enterprise plan includes dedicated support.",
]
query = "How quickly will my order arrive?"

for model_name, emb in [("small", small), ("large", large)]:
    doc_vecs = emb.embed_documents(docs)
    q_vec = emb.embed_query(query)
    sims = [cosine_sim(q_vec, d) for d in doc_vecs]
    top = max(range(len(sims)), key=lambda i: sims[i])
    print(f"{model_name}: top match [{top}] sim={sims[top]:.3f} | {docs[top][:50]}")

# Both should return doc 0; large may have higher absolute similarity scores.
# For most RAG use cases, text-embedding-3-small is sufficient.

Embedding a large corpus in a single API call will hit the per-minute token limit. The safe pattern is to batch documents in groups of 100–500 and apply exponential backoff on rate-limit errors. The function below handles both concerns and returns vectors in the same order as the input texts.

# Batch embedding with rate-limit handling
# The embedding API has per-minute token limits.
# For large corpora, batch documents and add backoff on rate limit errors.

import time
import openai
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_core.documents import Document

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")


def embed_with_retry(
    texts: list[str],
    batch_size: int = 100,
    max_retries: int = 5,
) -> list[list[float]]:
    """
    Embed texts in batches. On rate limit, back off exponentially.
    Returns vectors in the same order as the input texts.
    """
    all_vectors = []
    for start in range(0, len(texts), batch_size):
        batch = texts[start : start + batch_size]
        delay = 1.0
        for attempt in range(max_retries):
            try:
                vecs = embeddings.embed_documents(batch)
                all_vectors.extend(vecs)
                break
            except openai.RateLimitError:
                if attempt == max_retries - 1:
                    raise
                print(f"Rate limited. Retrying in {delay:.0f}s...")
                time.sleep(delay)
                delay *= 2   # exponential backoff
    return all_vectors


# Usage: embed 1000 chunks efficiently
chunks = ["chunk text " + str(i) for i in range(1000)]
vectors = embed_with_retry(chunks, batch_size=100)
print(f"Embedded {len(vectors)} chunks")

OpenAI's text-embedding-3 models support Matryoshka Representation Learning: the first N dimensions of a 1536-dim vector are themselves a valid (lower quality) embedding. Requesting dimensions=256 returns a 256-dim vector that uses 6x less storage and enables 6x faster approximate nearest-neighbour search while retaining roughly 95% of the full-dimension retrieval quality. This trade-off is worth taking for large vector stores where storage and search latency are constraints.

# Dimension reduction: OpenAI text-embedding-3 supports Matryoshka dimensions
# Reduce dimensions to lower storage cost and search latency.
# Quality degrades gracefully — 256 dims retains ~95% of 1536 dims quality.

from openai import OpenAI
import numpy as np

client = OpenAI()


def embed_truncated(text: str, dimensions: int) -> list[float]:
    """
    Embed with the native API to request truncated dimensions.
    LangChain OpenAIEmbeddings also supports dimensions= parameter.
    """
    response = client.embeddings.create(
        model="text-embedding-3-small",
        input=text,
        dimensions=dimensions,   # returns a vector of length `dimensions`
    )
    return response.data[0].embedding


def cosine_sim(a, b):
    a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b)
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b)))

query = "What is the refund policy?"
doc = "Items may be returned within 30 days of purchase with a receipt."

for dims in [256, 512, 1536]:
    q_vec = embed_truncated(query, dims)
    d_vec = embed_truncated(doc, dims)
    sim = cosine_sim(q_vec, d_vec)
    # Storage: dims * 4 bytes per vector (float32)
    storage_bytes = dims * 4
    print(f"dims={dims:4d}: sim={sim:.3f}  storage={storage_bytes} bytes/vector")

# dims= 256: sim≈0.87  storage=1024 bytes
# dims= 512: sim≈0.89  storage=2048 bytes
# dims=1536: sim≈0.91  storage=6144 bytes
# 256 dims is a good trade-off for large vector stores (6x storage reduction).
Mismatched models: indexing with text-embedding-3-large and querying with text-embedding-3-small produces garbage similarity scores. The model must be identical for indexing and querying.
Embedding empty strings: an empty chunk after cleaning produces a zero vector that matches nothing. Filter out empty or near-empty strings before calling embed_documents.
Truncation without warning: if a chunk exceeds the model's token limit, the API silently truncates it. Use the token counter to validate chunk sizes before embedding.

Re-indexing without flushing: if the vector store already contains old embeddings for a file and you re-embed with a different model, the collection becomes inconsistent. Always delete old vectors before re-indexing with a new model.

Bi-encoder models (the kind used for initial vector retrieval) encode the query and each document independently. This is fast but imprecise — the model never sees both texts together. A cross-encoder takes the query and a candidate document as a single concatenated input and produces a relevance score. This is far more accurate but 10–100× slower, making it only suitable for rescoring a small set of candidates already retrieved by the vector store. The two-stage pattern — retrieve 20 with ANN, rerank to 4 with a cross-encoder — is the standard approach for high-stakes RAG pipelines where precision matters.

# Cross-encoder reranking: re-score retrieved chunks with a more expensive model
# Bi-encoders (used for initial retrieval) trade precision for speed.
# A cross-encoder sees both the query AND the chunk together — much more accurate
# but too slow to run across an entire corpus. Use it only on the top-k candidates.

from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder
from langchain_core.documents import Document

# Load once at startup — ~100 MB, runs on CPU fine for < 20 docs
cross_encoder = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")


def rerank(query: str, candidates: list[Document], top_n: int = 4) -> list[Document]:
    """
    Score each candidate against the query using a cross-encoder, then
    return the top_n candidates sorted by descending relevance score.
    """
    if not candidates:
        return []

    pairs = [(query, doc.page_content) for doc in candidates]
    scores = cross_encoder.predict(pairs)   # returns a numpy array of floats

    ranked = sorted(
        zip(scores, candidates),
        key=lambda x: x[0],
        reverse=True,
    )
    return [doc for _, doc in ranked[:top_n]]


# Integration with a vector store retriever
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vectorstore = Chroma(persist_directory="./chroma_db", embedding_function=embeddings)

def retrieve_and_rerank(query: str, initial_k: int = 20, final_k: int = 4) -> list[Document]:
    """
    Two-stage retrieval:
    1. Fast ANN search retrieves initial_k candidates (high recall, mediocre precision).
    2. Cross-encoder reranks to final_k (high precision, slow — but only on initial_k docs).
    """
    candidates = vectorstore.similarity_search(query, k=initial_k)
    return rerank(query, candidates, top_n=final_k)


results = retrieve_and_rerank("What is the cancellation policy for enterprise subscriptions?")
for doc in results:
    print(f"Score-ordered result: {doc.metadata.get('source')} | {doc.page_content[:80]}")

The vector store is where embeddings live at query time. The right choice depends on corpus size, operational constraints, and whether you need transactional queries alongside semantic search. Chroma is zero-setup and works locally for development and small corpora. Pinecone is a fully managed service that scales to hundreds of millions of vectors without operational overhead. pgvector adds ANN search to an existing PostgreSQL database — the right choice when you already run Postgres and want metadata filters that join across relational tables. All three stores support the same LangChain retriever interface, so swapping them requires changing only the initialization code.

# Embedding storage strategies: trade-offs between HNSW, flat, and quantized indices
# The vector store choice affects recall, latency, memory, and cost at different scales.

# --- Strategy 1: Chroma (local, HNSW, no server needed) ---
# Best for: dev, prototyping, small corpora (< 1 M chunks)
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
chroma_store = Chroma(
    persist_directory="./chroma_db",
    embedding_function=embeddings,
    collection_name="kb_v1",
)

# --- Strategy 2: Pinecone (managed, serverless, enterprise scale) ---
# Best for: production, > 1 M chunks, multi-tenant isolation, zero-ops
from langchain_pinecone import PineconeVectorStore
import pinecone

pc = pinecone.Pinecone(api_key="PINECONE_API_KEY")
# Create index once (serverless, dimension must match embedding model)
if "kb" not in [i.name for i in pc.list_indexes()]:
    pc.create_index(
        name="kb",
        dimension=1536,       # text-embedding-3-small output dim
        metric="cosine",
        spec=pinecone.ServerlessSpec(cloud="aws", region="us-east-1"),
    )

pinecone_store = PineconeVectorStore(
    index_name="kb",
    embedding=embeddings,
    namespace="tenant_42",   # namespace per tenant for isolation
)

# --- Strategy 3: pgvector (PostgreSQL + vectors in one DB) ---
# Best for: existing Postgres stack, transactional + semantic queries together
from langchain_postgres import PGVector

CONNECTION = "postgresql+psycopg://user:password@localhost:5432/rag_db"
pgvector_store = PGVector(
    embeddings=embeddings,
    collection_name="kb",
    connection=CONNECTION,
    use_jsonb=True,   # store metadata in JSONB for fast filter queries
)

# Query with metadata filter (works identically across all three stores)
docs = pgvector_store.similarity_search(
    "employee vacation policy",
    k=6,
    filter={"department": "HR", "year": {"$gte": 2024}},
)
print([d.metadata["source"] for d in docs])

When documents change, re-embedding the entire corpus is expensive and slow. The correct approach is chunk-level deduplication: assign each chunk a stable ID derived from its source path and content hash, then only embed chunks whose ID is not already in the index. For source files that are updated, delete all existing chunks for that source and re-embed the new version. This reduces embedding API calls to exactly the diff between old and new content — critical when corpora update frequently or contain thousands of documents.

# Incremental embedding update: avoid re-embedding the entire corpus on each change
# Hash each chunk to detect what actually changed; only embed new or modified chunks.

import hashlib
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vectorstore = Chroma(persist_directory="./chroma_db", embedding_function=embeddings)
splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64)


def chunk_id(chunk: Document) -> str:
    """Stable ID: source path + positional hash of content."""
    src = chunk.metadata.get("source", "")
    return hashlib.md5(f"{src}::{chunk.page_content}".encode()).hexdigest()


def upsert_chunks(new_chunks: list[Document]):
    """
    Add chunks that are not already indexed; skip existing ones.
    For updates: delete old chunks by source, then call upsert_chunks.
    """
    existing_ids = set(vectorstore.get()["ids"])
    to_add = [c for c in new_chunks if chunk_id(c) not in existing_ids]

    if not to_add:
        print("All chunks already indexed — nothing to embed.")
        return

    ids = [chunk_id(c) for c in to_add]
    vectorstore.add_documents(to_add, ids=ids)
    print(f"Embedded {len(to_add)} new chunks (skipped {len(new_chunks) - len(to_add)}).")


def update_source(source_path: str, new_text: str):
    """Replace all chunks from source_path with freshly split + embedded chunks."""
    # 1. Delete old chunks for this source
    existing = vectorstore.get(where={"source": source_path})
    if existing["ids"]:
        vectorstore.delete(ids=existing["ids"])
        print(f"Deleted {len(existing['ids'])} old chunks for {source_path}")

    # 2. Split and embed new content
    doc = Document(page_content=new_text, metadata={"source": source_path})
    chunks = splitter.split_documents([doc])
    upsert_chunks(chunks)


# Example: first ingest
from pathlib import Path
text = Path("docs/policy.txt").read_text()
update_source("docs/policy.txt", text)

# Later: policy.txt is updated — only re-embeds that file
updated_text = Path("docs/policy.txt").read_text()
update_source("docs/policy.txt", updated_text)

Embedding converts text chunks into dense vectors where cosine similarity measures semantic closeness. Use text-embedding-3-small for most RAG pipelines — it gives the best cost-quality trade-off. For large corpora, batch in groups of 100–500 and apply exponential backoff on rate-limit errors. Use the dimensions parameter to request Matryoshka-truncated vectors when storage or search latency is a constraint. Always use the same model for indexing and querying, validate that chunks are non-empty before embedding, and check for truncation if your chunks approach the model's token limit.
